1: 'Then we turned and went up the road to Bashan' — the Bashan campaign immediately follows the Sihon victory (Deuteronomy 2:24-37). Og king of Bashan controlled the plateau and forest region northeast of the Sea of Galilee, modern-day Golan Heights and Hauran. Edrei (modern Der'a in southern Syria) was Og's fortified capital, strategically placed on the eastern plateau. The march 'up' reflects the ascent from the Jordan rift valley to the Bashan tablelands. Moses frames the campaign as the continuation of YHWH's program established in the Sihon victory — same divine guarantee, same military situation, same expected outcome.
2: 'Do not fear him, for I have given him and all his people and his land into your hand. Do to him as you did to Sihon' — the divine reassurance formula applied to Og establishes the Sihon victory as juridical precedent. 'Do to him as you did to Sihon' is not merely encouragement but a binding commission: what YHWH authorized and accomplished against Sihon's Heshbon establishes the herem-template for Bashan. The repetition of the formula (cf. Deuteronomy 2:24, 31; Deuteronomy 3:2) signals the suzerain's consistent authorization across multiple campaigns rather than ad hoc permission for each engagement.
3: The victory formula — 'so the LORD our God gave into our hands Og also ... and we struck him down until not one survivor remained' — mirrors the Sihon formula almost exactly (Deuteronomy 2:33). The identical language is deliberate: YHWH's pattern of warfare against Transjordanian kings follows a fixed template. In the suzerainty framework, the great king delivers the enemy; the vassal's army executes; the result is total. The phrase 'until not one survivor remained' is the herem-formula's outcome: the victory is understood as YHWH's claimed judgment, not merely military success.
4: 'There was not a city we did not take from them — sixty cities, the entire region of Argob' — the Argob region (modern Leja, a large basalt plateau in Hauran) was a geographically defined administrative unit within Og's kingdom. The number sixty is verifiable: Jair's renaming of the region's towns in Deuteronomy 3:14 reflects the administrative reality of a network of settlements. The hyperbolic flavor ('not a city we did not take') is the ANE conquest-annals idiom for comprehensive victory, attested in Egyptian and Assyrian campaign records that use the same rhetoric of total urban subjugation.
5: 'Cities fortified with high walls, gates, and bars, besides very many unwalled villages' — the distinction between fortified cities (ʿārîm bĕṣurôt) and unwalled settlements (ḥăṣērîm) reflects the standard ANE settlement hierarchy. Fortified cities had stone walls, bronze-sheathed gates, and heavy wooden bars; their conquest required siege equipment or divine intervention. Unwalled villages were agricultural dependencies of the city-center. The enumeration demonstrates that YHWH's gift encompassed both the military fortresses and the civilian population centers that sustained them.
6: 'We devoted them to destruction, as we had done to Sihon king of Heshbon, putting every city under the ban — men, women, and children' — herem (devotion to destruction) is the highest category of sacred warfare in the ANE: captured persons and possessions become irrevocably forfeit to the deity who granted victory. Hittite, Assyrian, and Moabite sources all attest similar concepts of total dedication of the defeated. The Mesha Stele (Moabite, 9th century BCE) uses the cognate term for Kemosh's commanded destruction of Israelite populations. Within Israel's theology, herem prevents cultic contamination from Canaanite religion entering the covenant community.
7: 'But all the livestock and the plunder from the cities we kept for ourselves' — the distinction between persons (subject to total herem) and livestock and goods (retainable as spoil) reflects the graduated scale of ANE sacred warfare. The Deuteronomic law distinguishes between distant cities (Deuteronomy 20:14: take women, children, livestock, plunder) and Canaanite cities (Deuteronomy 20:16-17: herem all that breathes). The Transjordanian kings fall into the herem category for persons but the spoil is legitimate booty — demonstrating that herem is the dedicating of human life to YHWH's judgment, not necessarily total annihilation of all material value.
8: 'At that time we took the land east of the Jordan from the two Amorite kings — from the Valley of the Arnon to Mount Hermon' — the Arnon to Hermon extent describes the complete Transjordanian plateau from the deep canyon that formed the Moab-Ammonite border (Arnon, modern Wadi Mujib in Jordan) to the snow-capped volcanic massif in the north (Hermon, 2,814m). This geographic bracket defines the entirety of what became Israel's Transjordanian holdings. The summary bracket frames the two campaigns (Sihon of Heshbon, Og of Bashan) as a single unified territorial acquisition.
9: 'Hermon is called Sirion by the Sidonians and Senir by the Amorites' — a parenthetical ethnolinguistic note attesting multiple ancient names for Mount Hermon. The Sidonians (Phoenicians) called it Sirion or Shiryôn; the Amorites called it Senir or Shenir. The Ugaritic texts (14th-12th century BCE) refer to Hermon as Ṣryn, closely related to Sirion. The multiple names reflect the mountain's significance as a geographical landmark visible across a vast region and claimed by multiple cultures as their own sacred or identifying peak. The Psalmist celebrates Hermon's dew in Psalms 133:3 as an image of covenant unity.
10: 'All the cities of the plateau, all Gilead, and all Bashan as far as Salecah and Edrei' — the geographic summary enumerates Og's kingdom in full. The plateau (miśôr) refers to the tablelands of Moab and northern Transjordan. Gilead is the forested hill country between the Yarmuk and Jabbok rivers. Salecah (modern Salkhad) was the easternmost city of the Bashan plateau, at the edge of the Syrian desert. The enumeration from south (the plain) through Gilead to the full extent of Bashan defines the territory distributed among Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh in the following verses.
11: Og's iron bed (9×4 cubits, approximately 4m × 2m) is noted as preserved at Rabbah of the Ammonites (modern Amman, Jordan), functioning as a display piece or royal trophy in the period of Deuteronomy's composition. The object is variously identified as: (1) a royal basalt sarcophagus (basalt is abundant in Bashan and appears black like iron), (2) an actual iron-framed bed demonstrating royal status, or (3) a cultic object. 'Rephaim' in the Transjordanian texts denotes a pre-Israelite population associated with exceptional stature (Deuteronomy 2:11, Deuteronomy 2:20; Genesis 14:5; Deuteronomy 15:20); in later Hebrew the word shifts to mean 'shades' — the dead in Sheol (Isaiah 14:9; Proverbs 9:18) — a fusion of giant-race and underworld-ancestor traditions. The defeat of Og as 'the last of the Rephaim' (Deuteronomy 3:11) is a deliberate literary closure: Israel's entry into the promised land eliminates the last survivors of the pre-Abrahamic peoples whose terror had paralyzed Israel at Kadesh (Numbers 13:33: 'we seemed to ourselves like grasshoppers').
12: 'This land that we took possession of — from Aroer by the Arnon River and half the hill country of Gilead with its cities — I gave to the Reubenites and Gadites' — the land distribution records begin with Reuben and Gad's southern portion. Aroer (modern Arair, on the north bank of the Arnon canyon) was the southernmost point of the allocation. The half of Gilead given to Reuben and Gad was the southern section below the Jabbok. This distribution reflects the original request of Reuben and Gad (Num 32) who had large livestock herds and preferred the Transjordanian pastureland to the more densely forested western territory.
13: 'The rest of Gilead and all Bashan, the kingdom of Og, I gave to the half-tribe of Manasseh. (The whole region of Argob in Bashan was called the land of the Rephaim)' — Manasseh's half-tribe received the northern portion: northern Gilead plus all Bashan. The Argob district (the large basalt Leja plateau) is identified as formerly Rephaim territory, reinforcing the continuity between YHWH's gift to Israel and the prehistoric displacement of earlier giant-peoples. Manasseh's Transjordanian settlement later became problematic — the Gileadite/Manassite territory east of the Jordan was among the first to fall to Assyria (2 Kings 15:29).
14: 'Jair the Manassite took all the region of Argob as far as the border of the Geshurites and the Maacathites. He named those villages Havvoth-jair — as it is still called today' — the naming of Bashan's villages after Jair reflects the ANE land-claim custom of eponymous naming: the conqueror's name applied to conquered territory signals ownership and memorializes the acquisition. Geshur and Maacah were small Aramean kingdoms at the northern boundary of Israel's Transjordanian holdings; they remained semi-independent (David later married a Geshurite princess, 2 Samuel 3:3). The 'as it is still called today' note signals the author's post-conquest vantage point.
15: 'To Machir I gave Gilead' — Machir son of Manasseh is the eponymous ancestor of the Machirites, who are associated specifically with Gilead throughout the tribal histories (Numbers 32:39-40; Joshua 17:1). This single-sentence allocation is an abbreviated cross-reference to the more extensive account in Numbers 32. The brevity signals that Moses's audience knew the tribal geography; the reminder in this context serves the retrospective framing of Deuteronomy 1-3 rather than providing new information about territory already settled.
16: 'To the Reubenites and Gadites I gave the territory from Gilead down to the Arnon Valley, with the middle of the valley as the boundary, as far as the Jabbok River, which is the Ammonite border' — the precise boundary description (mid-valley of the Arnon; the Jabbok as the Ammonite border) reflects the kind of boundary-treaty language attested in ANE land-grant documents. The Arnon canyon's middle line as the boundary suggests recognized natural-frontier diplomacy between Moabite territory (to the south) and Israelite territory (to the north).
17: 'The Arabah as well, with the Jordan as the western boundary, from Chinnereth down to the Sea of the Arabah, the Salt Sea, below the slopes of Pisgah on the east' — the Arabah (the Jordan valley) forms the western edge of the Transjordanian allocation. Chinnereth (the Sea of Galilee) in the north and the Salt Sea (Dead Sea) in the south define the longitudinal extent. The Pisgah slopes mark the eastern edge. The boundary description is precise enough to be verified archaeologically and matches the natural geographic features of the Jordan valley system.
18: 'All your fighting men shall cross over armed before your fellow Israelites' — the military obligation of the Transjordanian tribes to accompany the west-Jordan campaign was a formal covenant stipulation negotiated in Numbers 32. The phrase 'before your fellow Israelites' frames this not merely as military assistance but as the fulfillment of tribal solidarity within the covenant community. In ANE treaty law, vassals who received territory were obligated to render military service to the suzerain's ongoing campaigns; here YHWH's covenant functions as the suzerainty structure requiring cross-tribal military support.
19: 'But your wives, your little ones, and your livestock shall remain in the cities I have given you' — the distinction between combat-ready men who must cross and non-combatants who remain follows ANE military convention. The parenthetical 'I know you have much livestock' acknowledges the very reason Reuben and Gad had requested Transjordanian land (Numbers 32:1: much livestock). Moses cites the fact as recognition of their legitimate material interest in the land granted, not as criticism of their priorities. Leaving non-combatants also maintained claims to the newly won territory.
20: 'Until the LORD gives rest to your fellow Israelites as he has given rest to you' — the concept of mĕnûḥâ (rest, security) as the goal of the Canaan campaign is a key Deuteronomistic theological term. 'Rest' in this framework is not merely cessation of warfare but the establishment of settled, secure covenant life in the promised land — the completion of YHWH's program. The phrase 'as he has given rest to you' frames the Transjordanian allocation as already-realized rest, setting a precedent and creating obligation: those who have received must support those who have not yet received.
21: 'Your eyes have seen everything the LORD your God has done to these two kings' — the appeal to eyewitness experience of the Sihon and Og campaigns is Joshua's commissioning rationale. Joshua's authority is grounded not in abstract appointment but in observed divine action: he has personally witnessed YHWH's pattern of military deliverance. This is a standard ANE commissioning technique: the new leader's authority is established by appeal to the precedents he witnessed under the previous leader. The Sihon-Og victories become the juridical basis for the promise 'the LORD will do the same to all the kingdoms you are about to enter.'
22: 'Do not fear them, for the LORD your God himself fights for you' — the prohibition of fear (lōʾ tîrāʾûm) is the central psychological and theological requirement of Deuteronomic holy warfare. The phrase 'YHWH your God himself fights for you' (Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkem hû hannilḥām lākem) grounds courage in YHWH's direct military agency rather than Israel's numerical or technological superiority — a claim that runs throughout Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 1:30; Deuteronomy 20:4) and structures the subsequent conquest theology of Joshua.
23: 'And I pleaded with the LORD at that time' — Moses's intercession (wāʾetḥannan, from ḥānan, to seek favor, implore) is the verb of earnest petition used when the petitioner has no claim of right but only YHWH's mercy to appeal to. This is distinct from prayer grounded in covenant obligation; Moses frames his request as pure plea. The Second Temple tradition developed this moment extensively — Moses's prayer for entry into the land became one of the paradigmatic examples of rejected prayer in rabbinic literature (b. Sotah 13b), used to discuss why even the greatest intercessor's prayer can be refused.
24: 'O Lord GOD, you have only begun to show your servant your greatness and your mighty hand. What god is there in heaven or on earth who can perform deeds and mighty acts like yours?' — the incomparability formula ('what god is there ... who can perform deeds like yours') is one of the most common theological declarations in the Psalms and the Prophets (Psalms 35:10; Deuteronomy 71:19; Isaiah 40:18, 25; Micah 7:18). In the ANE polytheistic context, such declarations did not deny the existence of other divine beings but asserted YHWH's supremacy over them. Moses's prayer grounds the request in YHWH's demonstrated power — the Sihon and Og campaigns as evidence of incomparability.
25: 'Please let me cross over and see the good land beyond the Jordan — that good hill country and Lebanon' — the threefold description of the promised land ('good land', 'good hill country', 'Lebanon') clusters the land's desirability in terms of its fertility and elevation. The hill country refers to the central Judean and Ephraimite highlands; Lebanon's mountains would be visible from the northern portions of the land. The emotional weight of the request — Moses's lifelong goal is to see what he worked forty years to deliver Israel toward — makes its denial all the more significant as a theological statement about divine judgment.
26: Moses's exclusion from Canaan (Deuteronomy 3:23-27) is narrated as YHWH's refusal of his intercession: 'the LORD was angry with me on your account and would not listen to me.' The precise sin for which Moses is excluded is debated: Numbers 20:12 attributes it to his failure to 'believe in me' and 'uphold me as holy' at Meribah; Numbers 27:14 uses the same formula. In Deuteronomy the narrative reframes the exclusion through Moses's intercession and YHWH's flat refusal — 'Enough from you; do not speak to me of this matter again.' The Hebrew rav-lach (enough for you / it is too much for you) carries both sufficiency and rebuke. Moses is permitted to see the land from Pisgah but not enter — a distinction between visual possession and physical inheritance.
27: 'Go up to the top of Pisgah and look westward, northward, southward, and eastward. Look at it carefully with your eyes, for you shall not cross this Jordan' — Pisgah is the headland of the Abarim range in Moab, northeast of the Dead Sea. The four-directional panoramic vision granted to Moses is a symbolic land-grant: ANE land-grant documents sometimes granted land by symbolic sight as a legal act (cf. Genesis 13:14-15: 'lift up your eyes and look ... all the land that you see I will give to you'). Moses receives ownership by vision even without physical entry — the land is his to bequeath to Israel, though not his to inhabit.
28: 'Commission Joshua, and encourage him and strengthen him, for he shall cross over at the head of this people and shall lead them to inherit the land you will see' — the commissioning formula ('encourage him and strengthen him', ḥazzeq wĕʾammeṣ ʾōtô) is the formal language of leadership transfer in the ancient world (Joshua 1:6, 7, 9, 18 repeat the formula). Moses's personal relationship with Joshua — mentored since the Amalekite battle (Exodus 17:9) and aide at the tent of meeting (Exodus 33:11) — means the commission carries personal trust alongside formal authority.
29: 'So we remained in the valley opposite Beth-peor' — Beth-peor ('house of Peor', the Moabite deity) is the location of Israel's encampment before the Jordan crossing and the venue for Moses's farewell addresses. The site opposite Beth-peor (identified near modern Khirbet Ayun Musa) is associated with the Baal-Peor apostasy (Num 25) that Moses refers to at Deuteronomy 4:3. Situating Moses's covenant renewal speeches at the very place of Israel's most recent catastrophic failure makes the exhortations of chapters 4-30 all the more urgent — the geography is a standing reminder of the danger being warned against.
